title: Probate vs letters of administration - when each is actually required
p1:
Most people think probate is just paperwork after someone dies.
It isn’t.

What actually happens depends on one detail most people miss.
p2:
A common misunderstanding is that probate and letters of administration are interchangeable. They’re not.

Probate applies when there is a valid will naming an executor.
Letters of administration apply when there is no will, and the court appoints someone to manage the estate.

What surprises many people: without a will, you don’t ‘choose’ who handles things—the court does.
p3:
Both processes are the court’s way of giving legal authority to deal with assets like bank accounts, property, and debts.

The practical difference changes everything: timing, paperwork, and even who gets control.

If there’s a will, ensure the executor is ready. If there isn’t, expect additional steps before anything can be accessed or distributed.
conclusion: When dealing with an estate, did you assume probate always applies, or did you know letters of administration could be the deciding factor?
